summary: Apple issued a second silent macOS update to patch the Zoom webcam vulnerability affecting partner apps like RingCentral and Zhumu. Gruber addresses the irony that readers pointed out: Apple's silent, mandatory updates could themselves be considered 'nonconsensual technology,' the same term he used to criticize Zoom. He argues the situations are fundamentally different because macOS provides a user-accessible setting to disable automatic security updates, even if it's enabled by default. Gruber concludes Apple has struck a near-perfect balance between protecting most users automatically and respecting power users who want manual control.
key_points: Apple's second silent update addressed the Zoom webcam flaw in 11 partner apps including RingCentral and Zhumu, extending well beyond the original Zoom fix
Gruber confronts the apparent hypocrisy of calling Zoom's hidden web server 'nonconsensual' while praising Apple's silent updates, arguing the key difference is user control via a settings checkbox
The 'Install system data files and security updates' option is enabled by default but can be disabled, giving power users an opt-out that Zoom never provided
Gruber's one suggested improvement: silent updates should appear in the pending updates list for users who have disabled automatic installation
money_quotes: I've been using the phrase "nonconsensual technology" to describe Zoom's invisible web server that remained installed and running even after you deleted the Zoom app.
Clearly, the answer sounds like yes at first.
This option is enabled by default — even if you choose to install regular system updates manually — which is why the vast majority of Mac users are getting these "silent" updates automatically.

I think Apple has struck a nearly perfect balance here, between doing what's right for most users and doing what's right for power users who really do want to control when updates — even essential ones — are installed.
I also think Apple is doing the right thing by going to the press and explaining when they issue such updates.
key_insight: The difference between Apple's silent security updates and Zoom's hidden web server isn't about consent per se, but about whether users have a meaningful, discoverable way to opt out.
